title: #eng-runtime: 1768481504
preview:
Peter Holtz: kicking off a thread on graceful degradation during regional failover. need alignment on (1) load shedding response semantics (2) Retry-After + client behavior (3) streaming reconnect expectations when routing redirects.

Miles Thornton: +1. We saw in rehearsal that retries amplified the tail latency sp...
text:
Peter Holtz: kicking off a thread on graceful degradation during regional failover. need alignment on (1) load shedding response semantics (2) Retry-After + client behavior (3) streaming reconnect expectations when routing redirects.

Miles Thornton: +1. We saw in rehearsal that retries amplified the tail latency spike after the flip. We need something deterministic for the gateway/runtime.

Rafael Mendes: from oncall POV, the most painful bit is “is this a real regional impairment or are we self-DDOSing with retries?” If we standardize overload responses, we can tune burn alerts and the controller.

Vikram Singh: data plane angle: when a region is “yellow” (degraded but not hard down), we’ll still route some % there unless we have a shed mode. runtime needs to be able to say “nope, don’t queue me”.

Peter Holtz: current behavior in api-gateway is basically:
- timeouts -> 5xx
- client...

title: #eng-runtime: 1768429031
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: ok, need to land initial knob values for fast/standard/cost-efficient tiers this week. biggest open q: mixed prompt lengths + streaming. how are we preventing starvation if the queue is full of short prompts?

Sofia Ivanova: current proposal in the design doc is basically tier -> {max_wait_ms, max_bat...
text:
Sofia Ivanova: ok, need to land initial knob values for fast/standard/cost-efficient tiers this week. biggest open q: mixed prompt lengths + streaming. how are we preventing starvation if the queue is full of short prompts?

Sofia Ivanova: current proposal in the design doc is basically tier -> {max_wait_ms, max_batch_tokens, max_batch_reqs, prefill/decode split}. i’m worried it’s too hand-wavy.

Lena Fischer: pulled from the candidate sheet (w/ H100 80GB numbers). very rough but a starting point:
- fast: max_wait_ms=2, max_batch_reqs=8, max_batch_tokens=8192
- standard: max_wait_ms=6, max_batch_reqs=16, max_batch_tokens=16384
- cost-efficient: max_wait_ms=15, max_batch_reqs=32, max_batch_tokens=32768

Lena Fischer: but i think we should split prefill vs decode caps or we’ll get weird behavior on long prompts (prefill dominates the batch token budget and decode gets stuck behind it)

...

title: #eng-runtime: 1768432107
preview:
Noah Patel: pulling folks into this thread so we can lock metric names/labels for the prod perf canary. need enough to attribute regressions (queueing vs execution vs kernel) but not blow up cardinality.

Noah Patel: current canary emits e2e latency + tokens/sec per model. missing: runtime-only breakdown + scheduler...
text:
Noah Patel: pulling folks into this thread so we can lock metric names/labels for the prod perf canary. need enough to attribute regressions (queueing vs execution vs kernel) but not blow up cardinality.

Noah Patel: current canary emits e2e latency + tokens/sec per model. missing: runtime-only breakdown + scheduler queue time.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. for scheduler attribution we need queueing time and batch depth. otherwise every regression looks like “runtime slower” when it’s actually backlog.

Omar El-Sayed: what’s the target label set? I can export metrics from runtime + scheduler, but if we add `request_id`/`prompt_len` etc it’s going to be a dumpster fire.

Logan Wright: pls no `request_id` / `trace_id` / `prompt_hash` labels. histograms w/ a small set of stable dimensions only.

Nadia Rahman: also avoid free-form kernel names if they include hashes. if we need kernel attribution, ...

title: #eng-runtime: 1767995033
preview:
Samir Qureshi: heads up, I think we’ve got a nasty edge case: request cancel during KV relocation (compaction) can leak pages / sometimes wedge allocator health. Saw it once in staging + I can repro locally with the new compaction flag.

Samir Qureshi: symptoms:
- `kv_arena.free_pages` slowly trends down after burst...
text:
Samir Qureshi: heads up, I think we’ve got a nasty edge case: request cancel during KV relocation (compaction) can leak pages / sometimes wedge allocator health. Saw it once in staging + I can repro locally with the new compaction flag.

Samir Qureshi: symptoms:
- `kv_arena.free_pages` slowly trends down after bursts of cancels
- occasional `ASSERT failed: page.state == FREE` in debug build
- p99 spike when it happens (scheduler retries alloc)

Omar El-Sayed: ugh. do you have logs or a trace? which branch? pr-19357 codepath?

Samir Qureshi: yep, on top of pr-19371 + compaction enabled.
Here’s the debug log excerpt (trimmed):
```
I kv/relocate: begin seq=8421 layer=28 blocks=6 src=[0x1a20..0x1a25] dst=[0x2f10..0x2f15]
I scheduler: cancel requested seq=8421 reason=client_disconnect
I kv/seq: freeing pages for seq=8421 blocks=6
D kv/arena: free(page=0x1a20) state=MOVING -> forced_free
D ...

title: #eng-runtime: 1767480550
preview: Omar El-Sayed: Need eyes on the proposed eviction policy before I turn it into the ADR. Goal: when allocator hits pressure (or frag-trigger), we evict cached KV to free contiguous pages rather than thrash/oom. Proposal is tier-aware LRU (sequence-level) + prefix-cache interaction rules. Questions: pinned sessions, a...
text:
Omar El-Sayed: Need eyes on the proposed eviction policy before I turn it into the ADR. Goal: when allocator hits pressure (or frag-trigger), we evict cached KV to free contiguous pages rather than thrash/oom. Proposal is tier-aware LRU (sequence-level) + prefix-cache interaction rules. Questions: pinned sessions, and whether prefix KV should be in the eviction pool at all.

Samir Qureshi: can you paste the sketch of the policy? esp what you mean by “tier-aware” (Hosted vs Dedicated) and what object is LRU’d (seq vs block)

Omar El-Sayed: yup. current draft:
- Maintain LRU list per “eviction class” keyed by (tier, pool)
- Eviction classes ordered by priority: Free/Trial Hosted < Paid Hosted < Dedicated burst < Dedicated pinned < Private (no eviction unless explicitly enabled)
- Evict whole sequences’ KV allocations (not individual blocks) to avoid partial state
- Don’t evict ACTIVE se...

title: #eng-runtime: 1766009999
preview:
Alex Chen: Morning async — quick status:
- Done: landed kernel changes for "bucketed" batching (grouping by prefix hash instead of strict arrival). Observed ~12% throughput uplift on short-sequence mix.
- Blocker: 95th p99 tail on >2k tokens increased by ~30ms for a small subset of canary traffic. Possibly KV evicti...
text:
Alex Chen: Morning async — quick status:
- Done: landed kernel changes for "bucketed" batching (grouping by prefix hash instead of strict arrival). Observed ~12% throughput uplift on short-sequence mix.
- Blocker: 95th p99 tail on >2k tokens increased by ~30ms for a small subset of canary traffic. Possibly KV eviction interplay.
- Today: triaging eviction + warmup timing, will run a longer perf sweep at 10:30 PT and push a canary with conservative eviction thresholds.

Priya Natarajan: Nice work on bucketed batching. Quick thought — are we miscounting batchable windows when slot steal occurs?
If so, batches might be smaller in the tail causing more context switches.

Alex Chen: good call. I saw some slot-steal logs that align. Will add extra metrics for 'batchable_window_hits' and 'slot_steals_during_batch'.

Mateo Ruiz: I can run the longer perf sweep on the dedicated pool if you wan...

text:
Noah Patel: seeing a weird regression only under `quant_profile=q4_kvpack_v2` + long-context prompts. It *does not* reproduce on fp16 or `q8_dynamic`.

Noah Patel: symptoms: output goes off-rails after ~14-18k tokens (repeats / drops tool args), but only when we have KV cache enabled and we hit a few decode steps with batch>1.

Sofia Ivanova: is this on continuous batching path or single request?

Noah Patel: continuous batching. single-request decode (batch=1) looks fine even at 24k.

Grace Kim: which GPU SKU? A100 vs H100 can differ for q4 kernels.

Noah Patel: H100 SXM. repro not observed on A100 yet (haven’t tried hard).

Arjun Desai: `q4_kvpack_v2` is the new profile we wired for H100 in pr-19558, right? we changed baseline selection + some kernel prefs.

Omar El-Sayed: “KV pack v2” also changes how we lay out the KV blocks (and page size). long context is where it gets spicy.

N...

title: #eng-runtime: 1764613388
preview:
Rina Kobayashi: First full sweep is in ✅ (top models x {us-east, eu-west} x {H100, A100, L40S} where supported). This is the "we can run end-to-end" milestone, not a final baseline freeze.

High-level takeaways (numbers are *directional*, see tracker for exact):
• Chat streaming cohort (short prompt, variable decode...
text:
Rina Kobayashi: First full sweep is in ✅ (top models x {us-east, eu-west} x {H100, A100, L40S} where supported). This is the "we can run end-to-end" milestone, not a final baseline freeze.

High-level takeaways (numbers are *directional*, see tracker for exact):
• Chat streaming cohort (short prompt, variable decode): H100 is stable-ish on p50, but p99 is still noisy. Typical p50 TTFT ~150–220ms; p99 TTFT ~550–900ms depending on model + region. TPOT p50 is in the ~12–20ms/token band; p99 is what swings.
• Long-context prefill cohort (8–16k prefill, small decode): most of the pain is TTFT p99, and the gap between H100 and A100 is larger than we expected on a couple configs. Prefill-heavy runs are the most sensitive to placement + backpressure.
• Embeddings high-QPS cohort: throughput scales nicely, but we’re seeing occasional tail spikes that correlate with background GC / host content...

title: #eng-runtime: 1764196509
preview:
Claire Dubois: flagging a disagreement from yesterday’s sync: do we include prefix/KV caching in the Q1 baseline numbers or force everything “cold”? I’m worried we’ll bake in a config-dependent speedup that’s not comparable across runs.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. from harness POV, “cache on” is ambiguous unless we define ...
text:
Claire Dubois: flagging a disagreement from yesterday’s sync: do we include prefix/KV caching in the Q1 baseline numbers or force everything “cold”? I’m worried we’ll bake in a config-dependent speedup that’s not comparable across runs.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. from harness POV, “cache on” is ambiguous unless we define *exactly* what’s warmed (prefix cache vs per-session KV), hit rate target, and how long state persists. otherwise repro is hard.

Mateo Alvarez: but if we ship a baseline that’s always cold, it’s not representative for the dominant prod cohorts (multi-turn chat). folks will optimize the wrong thing.

Omar El-Sayed: it’s also two different mechanisms:
- prefix cache (shared / cross-request) affects prefill
- KV cache (per session) affects continuation across turns
We should not treat them as one checkbox.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduler interactions too. with warm KV, decode phase...

Marcus Reed: seeing a tail latency regression in perf-canary on H100 pool w/ `h100-balanced` preset enabled. p95 went +18-25% on streaming chat workloads (decode-heavy) vs baseline. anyone else looking at this?

Aisha Bello: yep, alert fired in #perf-canary. it’s isolated to builds with profile pack `0.3.1` + runtime `1.18.0-rc2`. p50 stable, p95/p99 up. tokens/sec roughly flat.

Sofia Ivanova: which gate? the “streaming_chat_p95” one?

Aisha Bello: gate is `h100_streaming_chat_p95_ms` (threshold +10%). we’re at +22% right now. dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/perf-canary/hw-profiles?var=sku:h100&var=workload:stream-chat (internal)

title: #eng-runtime: 1763978334
preview:
Benji Okafor: quick Q on profile loader edge cases before I lock the selection semantics: MIG partitions + multi-GPU nodes. Do we treat profile selection per *device* or per *node*? (and what do we do when devices differ)

Nina Petrova: depends on where we’re deploying. most of our dedicated pools are homogeneous no...
text:
Benji Okafor: quick Q on profile loader edge cases before I lock the selection semantics: MIG partitions + multi-GPU nodes. Do we treat profile selection per *device* or per *node*? (and what do we do when devices differ)

Nina Petrova: depends on where we’re deploying. most of our dedicated pools are homogeneous nodes, but we do have multi-gpu hosts (8x H100 SXM) and some oddball mixed configs in lab. MIG is the bigger gotcha.

Yuki Sato: +1. for MIG you can end up with `GPU 0: H100-SXM5-80GB (MIG 1g.10gb)` etc. SKU string is not stable. sometimes NVML reports the parent, sometimes only MIG device name.

Ethan Park: schema-wise we assumed `hardware.gpu_sku` maps to a single preset. if we go per-node and you have heterogeneous devices, we’d need either (a) disallow, or (b) define merge rules. merge rules sound like footguns.

Diego Santos: per-node is way simpler for deployment modes ...

Sofia Ivanova: For ENG-4826 (scheduler instrumentation): want to lock down the exact counters/tags today so Omar/Marcus can land the PR without us bikeshedding later. 

Sofia Ivanova: Key questions:
1) what’s required for baseline runs vs what’s “nice to have”
2) tags (high cardinality risks)
3) sampling + overhead expectations (prod safe)

Marcus Reed: From perf-suite POV I mostly need:
- queue_wait_ms (time spent waiting in scheduler before admitted)
- batch_assembly_ms (time to form batch once admitted)
- batch_size (distribution)
- dropped_requests / load_shed count
- “reason” buckets for drop (overload, max_queue, deadline)

title: #eng-runtime: 1763261084
preview:
Claire Dubois: we need to settle canonical defs for the suite before we lock the manifest schema. main confusion: TTFT = “first token on wire” vs “first token produced” (server-side), and TPOT w/ or w/o client overhead. can we decide measurement boundaries today?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. harness can do either, but if we...
text:
Claire Dubois: we need to settle canonical defs for the suite before we lock the manifest schema. main confusion: TTFT = “first token on wire” vs “first token produced” (server-side), and TPOT w/ or w/o client overhead. can we decide measurement boundaries today?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. harness can do either, but if we leave it ambiguous we’ll end up comparing apples/oranges across runs.

Sofia Ivanova: from runtime POV, I care about scheduler+decode behavior. “first token produced” (after queueing + prefill + first decode) is the clean signal.

Sean Gallagher: SRE POV: for release gating, we need something stable. client-side measurements can get noisy if the client can’t read fast enough, esp streaming.

Jonas Weber: also applied ML wants comparability across model variants. if TTFT includes network/client parsing, it’ll hide real runtime deltas.

Allison Grant: what’s the current SLO ...

Talia Benson: Proposed initial scope for v1:

text:
Talia Benson: Kicking off the “hardware tuning profiles pack” workstream. Goal: ship a versioned pack of safe-by-default runtime configs tuned per GPU SKU (H100 / L40S initially) + rollout/rollback guardrails. Can we confirm owners + where the draft schema doc lives?

Talia Benson: Proposed initial scope for v1:
- Profile schema + validation + loader + override order
- H100 + L40S tuned presets (scheduler/batching, kernel-selection toggles, KV cache / memory behavior)
- Benchmark suite updates + workload matrix + perf canary gates
- Docs: expected behavior + failure modes + rollout runbook
Non-goals (v1): new kernels from scratch, bespoke per-customer tuning.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: for scope. Kernels team can own H100 kernel-selection heuristics + any microbench-driven toggles we’re confident are safe to freeze. Suggest we keep “adaptive” knobs out of the profile unless we have hard...

text:
Sofia Ivanova: digging into yesterday’s mixed-workload behavior (long prompt prefill + short chat) and it looks like our backpressure thresholds are too forgiving for long-prefill. tail latency shoots up *before* admission control kicks in.

Julian Moore: do we have numbers? was this us-east canary or eu-west?

Sofia Ivanova: us-east canary. from the runtime side: when we get a couple of 12–20k prompt requests, they monopolize prefill slots and we start building scheduler lag for everyone.

Sofia Ivanova: quick snapshot from metrics (5m window during burst):
- `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95` went 45ms -> 650ms
- `runtime.queue_depth` flat-ish at ~40–60 (so the queue depth trigger didn’t fire)
- `runtime.prefill_inflight` pegged at max (8)
- `ttft_ms_p95` for “normal” chat (1–2k prompts) went 900ms -> 3.8s

Vikram Singh: that lines up with what routing saw. queue depth wasn’t huge, but ...

title: #eng-runtime: 1762319020
preview:

text:
Sofia Ivanova: aligning on the runtime metrics export for hot-route protection. routing folks are asking for queue depth + scheduler lag signals but we *cannot* blow up cardinality again 🙃

Julian Moore: yep. current WIP has per-(tenant, route, model, pool) tags which is… not gonna fly.

Vikram Singh: +1. needs to be low overhead + bounded tags. data plane can’t do high-card label sets on every enqueue/dequeue.

Mateo Alvarez: can we agree on the exact “contract” metrics that routing consumes? if we lock that, runtime can aggregate accordingly.

Logan Wright: for observability standards: anything used for automated admission should be stable + low-card. think region + route_group + tier + pool at most.

Allison Grant: also please no raw `customer_id` / `api_key_hash` tags in metrics. if we need tenant-level debugging, do it via logs/traces sampled, not metrics.

Sofia Ivanova: ok, pr...

title: #eng-runtime: 1759948420

Logan Wright: yep, saw it...
text:
Christine Wong: heads up from yesterday’s Sev2-ish blip: we had a failure mode that only hit streaming responses (SSE) — non-streaming / full responses were basically fine. want to make sure the status component split (Hosted API vs Hosted API - Streaming) can actually detect this cleanly.

Logan Wright: yep, saw it too. symptom: stream starts ok, then mid-stream we get a burst of disconnects/reset, tokens stop, clients retry. for non-streaming, request finishes before it hits the bad path.

Caleb Johnson: +1. looks like the runtime write loop was getting backpressured; we ended up timing out writes to the gateway and tearing down the stream. nothing wrong with model compute per se.

Mina Haddad: was this localized to a region or cross-region?

Christine Wong: mostly us-east, but a bit in eu-west. the confusing part was overall 5xx didn’t move much, so status page stayed “API Operatio...

title: #eng-runtime: 1755551234
preview:
Ethan Park: FYI: v3 /events is going to switch from newline-delimited JSON to an "envelope" binary framing (size-prefixed + optional compression). Old clients expect NDJSON. We need a compatibility shim plan for streaming + batch reads.

Lina Morales: Oof. Two main paths: gateway on-the-fly translator vs dedicated s...
text:
Ethan Park: FYI: v3 /events is going to switch from newline-delimited JSON to an "envelope" binary framing (size-prefixed + optional compression). Old clients expect NDJSON. We need a compatibility shim plan for streaming + batch reads.

Lina Morales: Oof. Two main paths: gateway on-the-fly translator vs dedicated streaming sidecar that handles framing and backpressure. Gateway path is simpler deploy-wise but risks adding tail-latency.

Ravi Singh: For batch reads we can also offer a cache-transform job that stores pre-unpacked NDJSON for hot tenants. That helps read-heavy workloads.

Ethan Park: Important constraints: preserve chunked streaming semantics for long-poll clients, maintain memory bounds (no unbounded buffering), and keep CPU cost predictable.

Priya Kapoor: Suggestion: opt-in header X-Redwood-Compat: ndjson to enable shim. Roll canary with 3 tenants first. We'll emit com...

text:
Omar El-Sayed: Seeing pretty ugly variance in p95 on the region sweep reruns. Same commit/config, 3 runs back-to-back.

Omar El-Sayed: Snapshot (A100 80GB, us-east):
- p50: 410-430ms (stable)
- p95: 980ms, 1.9s, 1.1s (not stable)
Throughput is within ~5%.

Rina Kobayashi: is that end-to-end including queue, or just model time?

Omar El-Sayed: e2e (client send -> last token). harness is closed-loop concurrency=32, but we *do* include server-side queue.

Noah Patel: p50 stable + p95 jumping smells like queue tail, not kernel perf.

Jonas Weber: +1. Also: are we mixing prompt shapes? the v3 prod mix has some long prefill + short decode, right?

Omar El-Sayed: yep. prompt_set=v3-production-mix. prefill distribution is wide.

Claire Dubois: can we check whether the runtime is switching kernels between runs? autotune can choose different fused-attn paths depending on observed shapes.

Noah ...

title: #eng-runtime: 1749044307
preview:
Diego Santos: quick q for folks thinking about autoscaling signals — with continuous batching on runtime, is external `queue_depth` actually stable enough as a scaler input for Dedicated? seeing cases where queue blows up then collapses when batcher flips modes.

Vanessa Ortiz: depends on what you mean by queue_dept...
text:
Diego Santos: quick q for folks thinking about autoscaling signals — with continuous batching on runtime, is external `queue_depth` actually stable enough as a scaler input for Dedicated? seeing cases where queue blows up then collapses when batcher flips modes.

Vanessa Ortiz: depends on what you mean by queue_depth. if it’s “pending requests in front of router” it’s *sometimes* ok, but if it’s “requests waiting inside runtime scheduler” it gets weird with long-prefill / decode split.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. queue depth can spike from a couple long-context prefill requests and looks like “need 10x replicas” even though decode side is underutilized.

Samantha Roy: we’re currently exporting `pending_requests` from fleet side (load balancer queue) + `inflight` from runtime. but the current autoscaler loop mostly keys on pending + p95 queue wait.

Diego Santos: yeah, the model matters too. ...

title: #eng-runtime: 1743435581
preview:
Omar El-Sayed: quick q for folks thinking about overload: do we want the primary saturation signal to be queue depth (prefill/decode) or token/sec saturation? trying to nail what we export from runtime for the breaker policy engine

Sofia Ivanova: both, but careful w/ semantics. queue depth is “pressure” and token/s...
text:
Omar El-Sayed: quick q for folks thinking about overload: do we want the primary saturation signal to be queue depth (prefill/decode) or token/sec saturation? trying to nail what we export from runtime for the breaker policy engine

Sofia Ivanova: both, but careful w/ semantics. queue depth is “pressure” and token/sec is “throughput ceiling”. they correlate but not always.

Lena Fischer: +1. queue depth catches backlog early. token/sec saturation catches “we’re running hot even if queue looks fine” (eg long decode).

Julian Moore: if we pick only one, queue depth is easier + less noisy. but it can false-trigger if batcher is doing work (queue spikes during batch formation).

Christine Wong: from gateway/policy side: we need something stable + explainable. if it’s noisy we’ll open/close breakers and create thrash.

Omar El-Sayed: right. token/sec saturation idea was something like `obs...

text:
Ben Carter: Uploaded profiling dump from this morning's A-load run. p95 up ~30% vs last week, p99 climbs when we hit microbatch=8. KV cache miss rate spikes in tail. https://artifacts/redwood/profiles/174250

Alice Nguyen: Can you paste the kernel-selection log? are we falling back to generic matmul at tail? :thinking:

Cheng Li: Quick summary: yes, we see kernel fallback after sequence len > 1024 + odd prompt shapes. batch=8 microbatching exposes head-of-line stalls. raw numbers:
```
- qps: 120 (stepped)
- gpu util: 78% avg, 95th burst 98%
- p95 latency: 180ms -> 235ms
- p99 latency: 420ms -> 710ms
- kv miss rate: 3% -> 12% at tail
```
Fallback log excerpt: `kernel_selector: no-opt-kernel -> generic_attention` repeated during bursts.

InfraBot: alert: gpu_util > 90% for group 'dedicated-prod' (3m window) at 1742502100 :rotating_light:

Ryan Patel: Two quick thoughts: 1) target_utiliz...

preview:
Noah Patel: We need to lock the backport policy risk rubric for the LTS line this week. Can we align here on: (1) what qualifies (Sev1/Sev2/security), (2) who has to approve, (3) what testing is mandatory before we merge into runtime-*-lts?

Mateo Alvarez: +1. Let’s keep it boring. Default should be “no backport” un...
text:
Noah Patel: We need to lock the backport policy risk rubric for the LTS line this week. Can we align here on: (1) what qualifies (Sev1/Sev2/security), (2) who has to approve, (3) what testing is mandatory before we merge into runtime-*-lts?

Mateo Alvarez: +1. Let’s keep it boring. Default should be “no backport” unless it meets clear criteria.

Connor O'Brien: Draft rubric proposal (trying to match what Release Eng can operationalize):
- Eligible classes:
 A) Security: CVE or Redwood Security advisory, or credible vuln w/ exploitability (even pre-CVE). Always eligible.
 B) Sev1: crash/data corruption/hard outage in production (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) OR “cannot serve” for a material customer segment.
 C) Sev2: customer-impacting correctness issue OR latency/throughput regression exceeding thresholds and confirmed not workload-specific.
- Non-eligible by default:
 - New features, be...

title: #eng-runtime: 1740862203
preview:
Omar El-Sayed: seeing something weird in the new cache hit-rate counters on the perf canary. under continuous batching, `kv_cache_hit_total` looks too high (like > requests) and the miss-reason breakdown doesn’t add up. anyone else seeing it?

Samir Qureshi: yeah I saw that in staging last night. looked like hits go...
text:
Omar El-Sayed: seeing something weird in the new cache hit-rate counters on the perf canary. under continuous batching, `kv_cache_hit_total` looks too high (like > requests) and the miss-reason breakdown doesn’t add up. anyone else seeing it?

Samir Qureshi: yeah I saw that in staging last night. looked like hits got attributed to the “wrong” request when multiple requests share a single prefill batch.

Julian Moore: +1. the scheduler emits a single prefill op for N reqs (continuous batch), then we split decode steps per req. if instrumentation is attached to the prefill op, it’s gonna smear across reqs.

Omar El-Sayed: exactly. I dug a bit and the counter increment is currently happening at `PrefillDone` with whatever `active_request_ctx` happens to be “first” in the batch.

Omar El-Sayed: repro: send 2 prompts, A repeated (should be hit), B unique (miss). in a batched prefill, both ...

text:
Samir Qureshi: Quick gut check on the proposed `wasted_prefill` metric in the PR. What exactly are we calling “wasted” here?

Samir Qureshi: If it’s “prefill tokens that didn’t contribute to subsequent decode due to misses/evictions” that seems super decoding-strategy dependent (spec decode / chunked prefill / streaming cancels etc). Worried we’ll ship a metric nobody trusts.

github-bot: PR opened: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/18424 (add-wasted-prefill-metric)

Omar El-Sayed: Yeah +1 to tightening the definition. The intent (at least from the runtime side) was: prefill work we did that never produced any user-visible output, i.e. request dies before we decode even 1 token.

Omar El-Sayed: Not “didn’t get reused by a later request” (that’s a different thing, and hard to attribute).

Noah Patel: Agree those are two different concepts.

Noah Patel: “wasted because cache churn...

title: #eng-runtime: 1740217732
preview:
Hiro Tanaka: heads up on the mixed FP8/INT8 path. we need stronger kernel selection guardrails before we let more profiles ship.

Hiro Tanaka: current issue: profile sets weights=int8 (per-channel) but activations fp8. on H100 this *usually* picks the right fused GEMM, but on L40S we’re sometimes falling into a “fal...
text:
Hiro Tanaka: heads up on the mixed FP8/INT8 path. we need stronger kernel selection guardrails before we let more profiles ship.

Hiro Tanaka: current issue: profile sets weights=int8 (per-channel) but activations fp8. on H100 this *usually* picks the right fused GEMM, but on L40S we’re sometimes falling into a “fallback” kernel that technically runs but blows up decode latency and occasionally OOMs at long ctx.

Noah Patel: +1. the selector is treating fp8 act as “supports flash-attn v3” but the int8 weight path doesn’t always have the same max seqlen coverage.

Grace Kim: can we just hard-cap context length for that combo? like `max_supported_seq_len` per kernel family and force policy fallback to baseline if request ctx > cap.

Omar El-Sayed: careful: cap needs to consider *prefill+decode* and KV cache growth. we saw: prefill at 12k ok, then decode pushes memory over on batch merge...
